title: Hanging a wallpaper border above a dado rail
subtitle: Add a decorative horizontal band with paste and accurate alignment
excerpt: How to hang a prepasted or paste-the-wall wallpaper border horizontally along a wall, positioned above a dado rail or at picture-rail height. Covers levelling, pasting, mitring at corners, and trimming at the ends.
sourceNotes: Practical Paperhanging, Leslie Hayward (1978). Collins Complete DIY Manual, Albert Jackson and David Day (1989). Wallpaper Manufacturers Association installation guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: A horizontal timber moulding fixed to a wall, typically at waist height (roughly 900 mm above the floor). Originally fitted to protect the wall plaster from chair backs. In modern interiors it is often used as a visual divider to separate two paint colours or to define the position of a wallpaper border.

A 45-degree join at an internal or external corner where two strips of border paper meet cleanly without overlap. Achieved by overlapping the two pieces, cutting through both at 45 degrees in one pass, and removing the waste from each layer.

content.text: A wallpaper border is a narrow strip of printed or textured paper that runs horizontally around a room. It sits above a

, at picture-rail height, or at the ceiling line. The job itself is similar to hanging full wallpaper: correct levelling, paste, and clean corner joins. Corners are the only technical difficulty: they need a
cut to avoid an ugly overlap.

Wallpaper border (measure the room perimeter and add 15% for joins and waste). Paste (or water if the border is prepasted; check the label). A paste tray or bucket. A sponge. A utility knife with a fresh blade and a metal straight-edge. A spirit level. A tape measure. A pencil. A damp cloth.

If the border sits above a dado rail, its bottom edge rests on the top of the rail moulding. Mark a light pencil line at this height on each wall using the spirit level as a guide; the dado rail itself may not be perfectly level along its full length. If no dado rail is present, decide on a height and mark a full level line all around the room.

Cut each length to the wall width plus 50 mm for trimming at each end. For a prepasted border, roll each length loosely, immerse it in a tray of clean warm water for the time stated on the pack (usually 30 seconds), then unfold it and hang. For a standard border, apply paste evenly to the back with a sponge or paste brush. Fold the border accordion-style and allow 2 minutes for the paste to soak in before hanging.

Starting at the least visible corner, press the border onto the wall with its bottom edge on the pencil line. Smooth with a damp sponge from the centre outward, working out any air. At the end of each length, overlap the next piece by 50 mm at the corner rather than cutting it short; the corner join is handled in the next step. Wipe any paste off the face of the border immediately with a damp cloth.

At an internal corner, let the first length lap around onto the next wall by the full border width. Hang the second length on the adjacent wall, overlapping the first. Place the metal straight-edge diagonally at 45 degrees through the overlap, corner to corner of the border width. Cut through both layers in a single pass with the utility knife. Remove the waste triangle from the top layer and peel out the waste from the bottom layer. The two mitred edges should butt cleanly. Press flat, smooth with a damp sponge, and run a seam roller along the join.

On a painted wall, a border can be repositioned within 30 minutes if it is slightly misaligned; peel back, adjust, and smooth. On a wallpapered wall, use overlap-and-match paste (a heavier adhesive) rather than standard paste, as the border must stick to a non-porous surface. If the border is very wide and heavy, let the first strip set for an hour before hanging the second to avoid the weight pulling the paper away from the wall.

attrs.symptom: Border lifts along its bottom edge
attrs.cause: The bottom edge is harder to press home, particularly where it sits on the top of a dado rail moulding.
attrs.fix: Apply a small amount of neat PVA or seam adhesive to the lifting edge with the tip of the filling knife. Press firm and wipe away any squeeze-out immediately.

attrs.symptom: Pattern repeat does not match at joins
attrs.cause: The lengths were cut without accounting for the pattern repeat.
attrs.fix: Borders have a stated pattern repeat on the roll. Always align the pattern at the end of one length with the start of the next before cutting, adding the repeat length to the wall measurement.
